title: User-Agent and Session Hardening
concept: Setting realistic browser headers, viewport sizes, and interaction delays to prevent false positives.

exercises.prompt: What is the main reason CAPTCHAs stop automated agents?

options.text: To prevent bots from misusing websites

exercises.explain: CAPTCHAs are used to prevent bots from misusing websites, ensuring that only real users can access certain features.

cards.title: Understanding User-Agent
cards.body: A User-Agent is like a name tag for your browser. It tells websites what browser and device you are using.

cards.title: Session Hardening
cards.body: Session hardening means making sure that your online activity is secure by setting realistic browser details and interaction speeds.

cards.title: Like a Disguise
cards.body: Think of User-Agent strings as disguises. Just like wearing a costume, setting realistic headers lets you blend in, preventing detection.

cards.title: Viewing Header Info
cards.body: When you check the network settings in your browser, you can see the headers it sends. This is where the User-Agent is shown.

cards.title: Myth: All User-Agents are the Same
cards.body: Not all User-Agents are equal; using a generic User-Agent can lead to being identified as a bot.

cards.title: Header Details
cards.body: Different browsers send different User-Agent strings.

left.text: Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0; Win64; x64) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/58.0.3029.110 Safari/537.3

right.text: Mozilla/5.0 (iPhone; CPU iPhone OS 10_3 like Mac OS X) AppleWebKit/603.1.30 (KHTML, like Gecko) Version/10.3 Mobile/14E5239e Safari/602.1

exercises.prompt: What should you keep in mind when setting your User-Agent?

options.text: It should always be the latest version
It should match the browser and device you are using
It doesn't matter as long as it has good features
It should always refer to a bot

exercises.explain: Your User-Agent should fit your actual browser and device to avoid being flagged as suspicious.

exercises.prompt: Using a generic User-Agent string is the best way to avoid detection as a bot.
exercises.explain: Using a generic User-Agent can actually make you more identifiable as a bot because it's commonly used by many automated tools.

exercises.prompt: To harden your session, you should set realistic browser _______ and interaction speeds.
exercises.sentence: To harden your session, you should set realistic browser headers and interaction speeds.
exercises.explain: The term 'headers' is what you need to set realistically to ensure proper functioning without raising flags.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following is an AI-generated User-Agent string?

options.text: Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0; Win64; x64) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/58.0.3029.110 Safari/537.3
Mozilla/5.0 (iPhone; CPU iPhone OS 10_3 like Mac OS X) AppleWebKit/603.1.30 (KHTML, like Gecko) Version/10.3 Mobile/14E5239e Safari/602.1

exercises.explain: The second option is specifically designed for Google’s bots, while the others belong to real user browsers.

exercises.prompt: To avoid being flagged as a bot, how should you set your browser's properties?

options.text: Set realistic headers, viewport sizes, and interaction delays
Always use the latest browser version

exercises.explain: Setting realistic headers, viewport sizes, and interaction delays creates a more genuine browsing experience, thus reducing suspicion of being a bot.
